file_name: NETGEAR,INC_04_21_2003-EX-10.16-AMENDMENT TO THE DISTRIBUTOR AGREEMENT BETWEEN INGRAM MICRO AND NETGEAR.txt

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement, including its attachment and order acknowledgments under the Agreement, constitutes the entire agreement between Distributor and NETGEAR with respect to the purchase, resale and distribution of the Products and is governed by the laws of the State of California except that body of law dealing with conflicts of law.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement, including its attachment and order acknowledgments under the Agreement, constitutes the entire agreement between Distributor and NETGEAR with respect to the purchase, resale and distribution of the Products.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the clause 'and is governed by the laws of the State of California except that body of law dealing with conflicts of law,' the contract creates ambiguity regarding governing law and potentially removes consumer protections afforded under California law. This is especially pertinent since both companies are located in California.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Commercial Code - Implied Warranty of Merchantability
perturbation.law_explanation: California Commercial Code § 2314 establishes an implied warranty of merchantability. By omitting the governing law clause, the agreement attempts to sidestep this legal requirement, exposing consumers to products that may not meet basic quality standards without legal recourse under California law. This is a direct contradiction because the original text confirms adherence to Californian law, while the modified text avoids explicit legal jurisdiction. Thus consumers may not have the rights granted to them under California law, which are pertinent given both Ingram and Netgear operate out of California.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, COM 2314 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Commercial Code - COM DIVISION 2. SALES [2101 - 2801] ( Division 2 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) CHAPTER 3. General Obligation and Construction of Contract [2301 - 2328] ( Chapter 3 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) 2314. (1) Unless excluded or modified (Section 2316), a warranty that the goods shall be merchantable is implied in a contract for their sale if the seller is a merchant with respect to goods of that kind. Under this section the serving for value of food or drink to be consumed either on the premises or elsewhere is a sale. (2) Goods to be merchantable must be at least such as (a) Pass without objection in the trade under the contract description; and (b) In the case of fungible goods, are of fair average quality within the description; and (c) Are fit for the ordinary purposes for which such goods are used; and (d) Run, within the variations permitted by the agreement, of even kind, quality and quantity within each unit and among all units involved; and (e) Are adequately contained, packaged, and labeled as the agreement may require; and (f) Conform to the promises or affirmations of fact made on the container or label if any. (3) Unless excluded or modified (Section 2316) other implied warranties may arise from course of dealing or usage of trade. (Enacted b
perturbation.justification: The omission of the governing law clause, particularly 'and is governed by the laws of the State of California except that body of law dealing with conflicts of law,' directly contradicts the implied warranty of merchantability as defined in California Commercial Code § 2314, as detailed in Scraped Law Snippet 1. By removing the specific reference to California law, the modified text creates ambiguity about which laws apply, potentially circumventing the consumer protections afforded under California law, which include the implied warranty of merchantability. Snippet 1 clearly states that a warranty of merchantability is implied unless excluded or modified, and removing the governing law clause can be interpreted as an attempt to exclude this warranty, which is a contradiction because it potentially leaves consumers without recourse if products are not merchantable. The original text ensured adherence to California law, including its consumer protections, while the modified text does not, creating a legally significant contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Following the [*], for any extension or renewal term, Distributor's appointment as Distributor shall be non-exclusive and NETGEAR may appoint other distributors in [*] at its sole discretion. 3. The terms and conditions of this Amendment, shall amend and supersede any conflicting terms of the original Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Following the [*], for any extension or renewal term, Distributor's appointment as Distributor shall be non-exclusive and NETGEAR may appoint other distributors in [*] at its sole discretion. 3. The terms and conditions of this Amendment, shall amend and supersede any conflicting terms of the original Agreement. All other terms of the original Agreement shall remain unchanged.
perturbation.explanation: The repetition of 'All other terms of the original Agreement shall remain unchanged.' after stating that the amendment supersedes any conflicting terms introduces an internal contradiction. While not directly violating a specific consumer protection law, it creates legal ambiguity that could be exploited to limit distributor rights or consumer protections. This increases the legal risk that consumer protections may not be correctly applied.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 1654 states that in cases of ambiguity, the language of a contract should be interpreted most strongly against the party who caused the uncertainty to exist. The introduction of a contradiction creates uncertainty. Omitting explicit language about consumer rights is an implicit attempt to weaken them, which is in conflict with California's principle of construing ambiguities against the drafter.

OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 3. INTERPRETATION OF CONTRACTS [1635 - 1663] ( Title 3 enacted 1872. ) 1654. In cases of uncertainty not removed by the preceding rules, the language of a contract should be interpreted most strongly against the party who caused the uncertainty to exist. (Amended by Stats. 1982, Ch. 1120, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The added sentence 'All other terms of the original Agreement shall remain unchanged' contradicts the previous statement that the amendment supersedes any conflicting terms of the original agreement. This introduces ambiguity about which terms of the original agreement still apply. California Civil Code § 1654, as shown in Scraped Law Snippet 1, states that in cases of uncertainty, the language of a contract should be interpreted most strongly against the party who caused the uncertainty. This contradiction creates uncertainty and therefore violates the law.

perturbation.original_text: NETGEAR reserves the right to add Products to the Price List at its sole discretion and any such additional Products shall be offered to Distributor at discounts to be determined at that time.
perturbation.changed_text: NETGEAR reserves the right to add Products to the Price List at its sole discretion.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'and any such additional Products shall be offered to Distributor at discounts to be determined at that time,' NETGEAR could potentially offer new products without any pricing agreement, potentially leading to unfair pricing for distributors. While not a direct consumer law violation, unfair pricing for distributors can ultimately lead to increased prices or reduced service for consumers.
perturbation.law_explanation: California's Unfair Competition Law (UCL) prohibits unfair or fraudulent business acts or practices. While not explicitly consumer-focused, predatory or unfair pricing towards distributors can be considered an unfair business practice under the UCL. By reserving the right to add products without guaranteed discounts, NETGEAR creates a scenario where it could unfairly disadvantage distributors, ultimately affecting consumers through higher prices or reduced availability. The omitted text provided a framework for fair negotiation, while its absence opens the door to potentially unfair practices.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 17200 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 5. Enforcement [17200 - 17210] ( Chapter 5 added by Stats. 1977, Ch. 299. ) 17200. As used in this chapter, unfair competition shall mean and include any unlawful, unfair or fraudulent business act or practice and unfair, deceptive, untrue or misleading advertising and any act prohibited by Chapter 1 (commencing with Section 17500) of Part 3 of Division 7 of the Business and Professions Code. (Amended by Stats. 1992, Ch. 430, Sec. 2. Effective January 1, 1993.)
perturbation.justification: The original contract includes a clause that new products will be offered to the distributor at a discount to be determined at that time. The changed text removes this clause. The Perturbation explanation states that by removing this clause, it can potentially lead to unfair pricing for distributors. The Law Explanation states California's Unfair Competition Law (UCL) prohibits unfair business acts or practices. Scraped Law Snippet 1 supports this, stating that unfair competition shall mean and include any unlawful, unfair or fraudulent business act or practice. Therefore, the omission of the clause regarding discounts for distributors potentially contradicts California's Unfair Competition Law, because it opens the door to unfair pricing for distributors.
